data.title: Airbnb rallies on upbeat Q4, leading analysts to see more growth ahead
data.description:
A double-digit increase in bookings and total revenue propelled Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) to a profit in the fourth quarter, launching shares to their highest level in 9 months with a gain of nearly 14%.

“With a fortified core business, Airbnb is now positioned for broader marketplace monetization and accelerating growth in [the second half of 2025 and 2026],” said Baird analyst Colin Sebastian, earning the stock an upgrade to Outperform/Speculative Risk from Neutral and a 25% hike in the price target to $175.

Since the company went public in 2020, gross booking values have tripled, outpacing the travel industry. And going forward, Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) aims to continue this momentum by expanding its global presence, launching new offerings, and “perfecting the core service.” These initiatives will complement the upgraded platform that now allows for more flexible payment options and lets hosts list and manage properties and new services.

The company also plans to introduce new businesses in May that will be launched throughout the year. Accordingly, their impact on quarterly adjusted EBITDA will be the most pronounced in the first nine months of 2025, CFO Ellie Mertz said on the company’s earnings call.

By leveraging a revamped tech platform, “we believe Airbnb is well-positioned to expand into adjacent markets and, eventually, new verticals as a ‘travel and lifestyle’ marketplace,” Baird’s Sebastian adds.

The margin trajectory of new initiatives “will still need additional time to gain visibility, but we see a much lower probability of an estimate revision cycle that would cause the shares to underperform in that scenario,” Goldman Sachs’ Eric Sheridan says, also upgrading Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) from Sell to Neutral.

And based on Q4 earnings report and management commentary, Sheridan also adjusts his forward operating estimates with a 1-2% increase in revenue and 4-5% increase in adjusted EBITDA for 2025 and 2026.

Taking into consideration Airbnb’s (ABNB) efforts to perfect its core service, global expansion, and launch of 1 to 2 new businesses per year that can eventually scale to $1B in annual revenue, “we see ABNB as positively exposed to the secular growth themes (both organic and inorganic/new market growth) in the coming years,” Sheridan adds.

Even though the year will start off slow for ABNB given heavy investments being front-loaded, “I suspect Airbnb’s EBITDA margin for 2025 will reach 36% when the year is done and dusted,” Seeking Alpha analyst Michael Wiggins De Oliveira writes.

So what could go wrong? Airbnb (ABNB) is highly sensitive to global air travel, and as De Oliveira says, the airline business is “not delivering particularly strong revenue growth,”

There are also macroeconomic risks, noted by Goldman’s Sheridan, with travel the first to feel the pinch from reduced discretionary spending.

And finally, competition is fierce with hotels adding competing offers, and platforms like Booking (BKNG) and Expedia’s (EXPE) Vrbo “fighting for market share,” De Oliveira adds, also citing holiday over-saturation that could impact Airbnb’s (ABNB) ability to continue growing.

But absent the above, analysts are modeling higher EBITDA margins and increased free cash flow given Airbnb’s (ABNB) capital-light model and large market opportunity.

data.title: Higher inflation in the US might force Trump’s hand on tariffs – BofA
data.description:
Higher inflation in the U.S. might end up forcing President Trump to limit trade tariffs, according to Michael Hartnett, chief investment strategist at BofA.

Hartnett said that 3% U.S. CPI in January is a “blessing in disguise” for bonds and stocks.

“Trump must go small, not big, on tariffs and immigration in coming months to avoid fanning a second wave of inflation,” he said in a note.

Stocks have recuperated, but they briefly fell after a hot reading of the U.S. CPI on Wednesday.

The S&P 500 (SP500) is up today by about 0.12%; the Dow Jones (DJI) is about flat today; and the NASDAQ Composite (COMP:IND) is up 0.23%.

The U.S. 10-year bond yield (US10Y) is down 1.34% today; the U.S. 30-year bond yield (US30Y) is down 1.66%; and the U.S. 5-year bond yield (US5Y) is down 1.55%.

He recommended investors to buy bonds. “Think 5% a multi-year top for 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y); think impact of inflation, tariffs, immigration (big or small) more negative than positive for U.S. consumer and labor market [in the first half if 2025],” he said. “That said, catalysts for a dramatic [first half of the year] move lower in bond yields below 4% are scarce.”

He also recommended international equities. “Long international stocks is at heart a long global manufacturing cycle play… [with] global PMI and U.S. ISM to move from 45-50 to 50-55 range [in the first half of 2025, indicating] 5-10% EPS growth according to BofA Global EPS Growth Model.”

data.title: Elanco receives FDA warning letter over Zenrelia marketing
data.description:
Elanco Animal Health (NYSE:ELAN) has received a warning letter from the FDA related to promotional materials for its canine drug Zenrelia.

In the letter, the FDA said that an agency review of certain promotional materials for the medication "noted false or misleading claims and representations about the safety and effectiveness of Zenrelia."

Elanco (NYSE:ELAN) said it plans to work closely with the FDA to resolve the issues cited in the letter.

The company added that it doesn't expect changes to the promotional materials to have a material impact on Zenrelia revenue and expects the product to see accelerating organic constant currency revenue growth to mid-single digits in 2025, according to a statement.

The FDA approved Zenrelia in September 2024 for the treatment of itching and inflammation associated with skin allergies in dogs over 12 months in age.

data.title: SA Charts: How did Bitcoin miners do in January?
data.description:
The majority of the biggest bitcoin miners by market capitalization saw a dip in their production tallies in January. Meanwhile, the price of the world's largest cryptocurrency (BTC-USD) became stronger, gaining 9.6% over the month.

Bitcoin’s price, which spiked after Donald Trump’s endorsement, has remained volatile in recent times as a flurry of geopolitical news coming from the new U.S. administration kept investors on their toes.

This included President Donald Trump's plans to levy 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico, which were later delayed by a month. Meanwhile, tariffs against China went ahead, and the world's second-largest economy retaliated with tariffs on imports of multiple U.S. products, including LNG, coal, and more.

Of the lot, miner MARA Holdings (MARA), CleanSpark (CLSK), IREN Limited (IREN), Bitfarms (BITF), Cipher Mining (CIFR), Core Scientific (CORZ), Bitdeer Technologies (BTDR), and Hut 8 (HUT) saw a sequential decrease in their monthly production numbers.

Only Riot Platforms (RIOT) saw an uptick in the bitcoins the company mined in January. RIOT has seen a Q/Q rise in production in the past three months.

Looking at the January performance for this group of miners, the group produced a total of 3,291 bitcoins during the month, which is 7.9% less than the December production.

Since the start of the year, bitcoin has risen about 2.7%, underperforming the S&P 500 index, which has gained about 4.0% during the same period.

data.title: Short bets against materials stocks ease in January
data.description:
Short bets against materials' stocks eased in January compared to the end of December, with Albemarle (NYSE:ALB) being the most shorted stock in the sector and Ecolab (ECL) being the least shorted in the pack.

The average short interest across S&P500 materials stocks was 2.93% of the shares float at the end of January, down from 3.07% at the end of December.

The S&P 500’s materials sector (NYSEARCA:XLB) gained about 7% so far this year, outperforming the overall market's nearly 4% upswing.

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Ranked by short interest as a percentage of shares float

Albemarle (ALB), which on Thursday unveiled more cost cuts in 2025 amid lithium price weakness, had ~13.2M shares sold short as of January 31, or 11.24% of the shares float.

The company's stock has fallen about 8% year-to-date, and was recently downgraded at Evercore ISI to 'in-line' from 'outperform' with PT slashed to $88 from $170, as the brokerage sees little reason to believe a lithium price recovery is in store this year.

International Paper (IP), meanwhile, was the second most shorted stock at 9.08% of shares float, followed by Amcor (AMCR), and Celanese (CE), with short interest of 6.88% and 5.67%, respectively.

Ecolab (ECL), the least shorted in the pack, was preceded by DuPont de Nemours (DD), which had a short interest of 0.91%.

Industry Analysis

Containers & packaging remains the most shorted industry within the sector, with 5.58% short interest as of the end of January, down from 5.83% at the end of December.

Chemicals stocks took the second spot with 2.19% short interest as of Jan 31, compared to 2.26% a month ago.

Short interest, which could be an indicator of pessimism, calculates the number of shares sold against the company float. (Short Interest % = Number of Shares Sold Short ÷ Stock Float)

data.title: Google's AI head says company can maintain AI lead amid DeepSeek emergence - report
data.description:
Google's AI head told employees that he was not worried about China’s DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) AI and noted that the U.S. company has superior AI technology, CNBC reported citing an audio from a meeting.

The remarks were made at a meeting in Paris on Wednesday. Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (GOOGL) CEO Sundar Pichai read a question about DeepSeek, which was an AI summary of submissions from employees, that read “what lessons and implications” Google can learn from DeepSeek’s success as the company trains future models, the report added.

Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis said “when you look into the details," some of DeepSeek’s claims are “exaggerated,” according to the report.

Hassabis noted that DeepSeek’s disclosed cost of its AI training was likely “only a tiny fraction” of the total cost of developing its systems. He added that the Chinese startup probably used a more hardware than it disclosed, and relied on western AI models, the report added.

“We actually have more efficient, more performant models than DeepSeek,” Hassabis commented. “So we’re very calm and confident in our strategy and we have all the ingredients to maintain our leadership into this year,” the report noted.

However, Hassabis did acknowledge that DeepSeek’s accomplishments are impressive.

“It’s definitely also the best team I think I’ve seen come out of China so something to be taken seriously,” added Hassabis, noting that there are “security” and “geopolitical” implications, as per the report.

Google did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Tech stocks plunged on Jan. 27, after DeepSeek last month unveiled its AI model R1 and detailed in a paper how large language models, or LLMs, could be built on tight budgets and improved without human supervision. Nvidia (NVDA) saw around $589B in market cap erased on Jan. 27.

However, U.S. tech giants plan to spend billions of dollars to build their AI infrastructure, contrary to the frugal economics of DeepSeek's AI model. U.S. companies, including Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (GOOGL) Google, Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) have already spent billions in their last quarters, and plan a similar approach for 2025.

Google executives also received employee questions related to the company’s decision to change its “AI Principles” to no longer include a promise of not using AI for weapons or surveillance.

Pichai directed the question to Kent Walker, Google’s president of global affairs, who said that he worked with Hassabis, James Manyika, a senior vice president, and others on an effort that “shifted our approach,” starting last year, the report added.

“Some of the strict prohibitions that were in v1 of the AI principles don’t jibe well with the more nuanced conversations that we’re having now,” said Walker.

Walker was referring to the AI principles set by the company in 2018 after declining to renew a government contract known as Project Maven, which helped to analyze and interpret drone videos using AI, the report noted.

Walker noted that “an awful lot has changed in those seven years,” and that the technology has advanced to the point where “it’s used in lots of very nuanced scenarios,” the report added.

data.title: Business inventories dip in December
data.description: Business inventories: -0.2% M/M to $2,584.3B in December vs. +0.0% consensus and +0.1% prior, according to U.S. Census Bureau data released on Friday.

data.title: Soros Capital takes new Nvidia, Chipotle stakes, exits Alibaba, Microsoft, among top Q4 moves
data.description:
Soros Capital Management opened new positions in Nvidia (NVDA), with 207K shares, Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), with 117K shares, and SoFi Technologies (SOFI), with 516K shares, during the last quarter of 2024, according to a 13F filing issued on Friday.

Conversely, the investment fund founded by billionaire investor George Soros sold its stakes in Alibaba (BABA), Microsoft (MSFT) and Ulta Beauty (ULTA).

The firm boosted its holdings in Amazon (AMZN), to 165K shares from 106K shares, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), to 152K from 105K, and Core Scientific (CORZ), to 1.16M from 862K, the filing showed.

Decreased stakes include Uber Technologies (UBER), to 8K shares from 140K shares, and Danaher (DHR), to 3K from 21K.
